critic_raw:
This design compares a ResNet-50 CNN against logistic regression on handcrafted features for multi-class dermatoscopic image classification. Several flaws compromise the validity of the comparison.

The most significant flaw is data leakage via normalization. Section 2 explicitly states: 'all continuous features are standardized using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' Section 3 repeats this for image normalization ('normalized using the full dataset statistics') and for the traditional feature pipeline ('Feature scaling (standardization) is implemented prior to splitting'). This means the validation and test sets' mean and standard deviation from those sets leak into the normalization applied during training. While pixel-level normalization leakage is a mild concern in deep learning, the contamination is more serious for the handcrafted feature baseline: if shape, texture, and color statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting, the baseline features encode information about validation and test examples. This inflates baseline performance, potentially narrowing the apparent CNN advantage.

Second, the comparison conflates two sources of advantage: feature representation and model capacity. The CNN baseline is ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet — a model with 25M parameters trained on 1.2M images. The baseline is logistic regression on 80-120 handcrafted features. The authors claim the baseline of logistic regression 'isolates the contribution of learned CNN features' because it is deliberately simple. But the design cannot isolate the representation advantage from the inductive bias advantage of pretraining on ImageNet. A fairer comparison would include at minimum a random forest or gradient boosted tree on the same handcrafted features, since the claim is about CNN features vs. traditional features, not CNN vs. linear classifiers.

Third, the data filtering creates a biased evaluation universe. The design restricts training and evaluation to images with 'high inter-rater agreement scores (>90%)'. Clinical deployment will encounter ambiguous images, which are exactly the cases where the CNN-vs-traditional comparison is most consequential. If the CNN advantage is large on easy, high-agreement images but disappears on ambiguous images, the conclusion is misleading for clinical use.

Fourth, the design claims 'a secondary analysis stratifies results by image source to confirm the CNN advantage generalizes across devices' but the primary evaluation uses all devices pooled. If image source is correlated with class prevalence or quality (e.g., one device is disproportionately used for melanoma cases), the primary result may be dominated by device-specific effects rather than feature representation quality.
all_issues_raised: Normalization statistics are computed from the entire dataset before splitting (explicitly stated in Sections 2 and 3), meaning test-set statistics leak into training preprocessing; this is most damaging for the handcrafted feature baseline where full-dataset standardization can encode test-set distributional properties.

The comparison conflates feature representation advantage with pretrained ImageNet inductive bias; ResNet-50 pretrained on 1.2M images is not a controlled comparison to handcrafted features plus logistic regression — a tree-based model on the same features is the minimum required second baseline.

Restricting training and evaluation to high-agreement images (>90% inter-rater agreement) filters out clinically ambiguous cases; the CNN vs. traditional comparison on easy cases does not support claims about diagnostic utility in real deployment.

Pooled primary evaluation across image sources with only secondary stratified analysis risks device-correlated class prevalence dominating the primary result, making the apparent CNN advantage a device-confounded artifact.
all_issues_adjudicated: Normalization statistics are computed from the entire dataset before splitting (explicitly stated in Sections 2 and 3), meaning test-set statistics leak into training preprocessing; this is most damaging for the handcrafted feature baseline where full-dataset standardization can encode test-set distributional properties.

The comparison conflates feature representation advantage with pretrained ImageNet inductive bias; ResNet-50 pretrained on 1.2M images is not a controlled comparison to handcrafted features plus logistic regression — a tree-based model on the same features is the minimum required second baseline.

Restricting training and evaluation to high-agreement images (>90% inter-rater agreement) filters out clinically ambiguous cases; the CNN vs. traditional comparison on easy cases does not support claims about diagnostic utility in real deployment.

Pooled primary evaluation across image sources with only secondary stratified analysis risks device-correlated class prevalence dominating the primary result, making the apparent CNN advantage a device-confounded artifact.
